Term Description Typical trigger WateringEvent A single irrigation event — timestamp, volume, source Manual entry or watering schedule confirmation FeedingEvent A fertilization/irrigation event with nutrient data (EC, pH, dosages) Fertilization according to a nutrient plan (REQ-004) WateringLog Unified log — aggregates WateringEvents and FeedingEvents in a single view Aggregated automatically

Difference from FeedingEvents

FeedingEvents document irrigations where fertilizer was added — with the full nutrient profile (target EC, target pH, product dosages). The WateringLog shows both types side by side, so you can follow the irrigation history without switching context.

docs.title: Opening the Watering Log
docs.text:
1. Navigate to a planting run or plant.
  2. Click the Watering Log (or Irrigation) tab.
  3. The log view shows all irrigation events in chronological order.

Screenshot pending

This screenshot will be added in a future version.

docs.title: What the Watering Log Displays
docs.text:
Each entry shows the following information:

Field Description Date & Time Timestamp of the irrigation event Volume (liters) Irrigation volume in liters EC Electrical conductivity of the nutrient solution (if fertilizer was used) pH pH of the irrigation solution (if recorded) Type Irrigation or Fertilization Source Manual, watering schedule, automatic Note Optional free-text annotation

docs.title: Entering a Watering Event Manually
docs.text:
1. Click New Watering Event.
  2. Enter the volume and timestamp.
  3. Optionally add EC, pH, and a note.
  4. Click Save.

Use a watering schedule

If you have configured a watering schedule (WateringSchedule) for your planting run, Kamerplanter automatically creates tasks for due irrigations. Confirming these tasks automatically records the events in the log — no manual entry required.

docs.title: Watering Log and Nutrient Planning
docs.text:
The WateringLog is closely integrated with the fertilization logic (REQ-004):

  • EC trend across multiple irrigation events is visible as a mini-chart in the log view (when data is available).
  • Flush detection: When EC and pH are recorded, Kamerplanter can automatically flag flushing events.
  • Runoff analysis: When runoff EC is recorded, nutrient accumulation in the substrate can be detected.

docs.title: Frequently Asked Questions
docs.text:
Are automatic irrigations (Home Assistant) also logged?

Yes. When Kamerplanter receives irrigation data via the Home Assistant integration, it is automatically recorded as WateringEvents in the log — with the source set to automatic.

How long are watering log entries retained?

Watering log entries are stored according to the data retention policy (NFR-011). By default, raw entries are kept for 90 days, after which they are consolidated into daily aggregates.

Can I correct entries in the log after the fact?

Yes. Click an entry and select Edit. Changes are recorded with a timestamp.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Fertilization Logic — Nutrient plans and FeedingEvents (REQ-004)
  • Planting Runs — Configuring a watering schedule
  • Tank Management — Irrigation tanks and fills

docs.title: ADR-003: Authlib instead of python-jose for JWT
docs.text: Status: Accepted Date: 2026-02-01 Deciders: Kamerplanter Development Team

docs.title: Context
docs.text: A Python library was needed for JWT creation and validation as well as OAuth2/OIDC flows. python-jose was the previous standard recommendation for FastAPI.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Authlib replaces python-jose for all JWT and OAuth2/OIDC operations.

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: python-jose has been unmaintained since 2022 and has known security vulnerabilities. Authlib is actively maintained, supports modern JWT standards (RFC 7519), and provides complete OAuth2/OIDC client implementations.

docs.title: Positive
docs.text:
• Actively maintained library with regular security updates
  • Full OAuth2/OIDC support for Google, GitHub, Apple

docs.title: Negative
docs.text: • Different API than python-jose — migration required changes to TokenEngine

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Authlib Documentation
  • REQ-023: User Management & Authentication

docs.title: ADR-004: bcrypt directly instead of passlib
docs.text: Status: Accepted Date: 2026-02-01 Deciders: Kamerplanter Development Team

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: bcrypt is used directly, without passlib as an abstraction layer.

docs.title: Rationale
docs.text: passlib 1.7.4 is incompatible with bcrypt 5.x (current). Since Kamerplanter uses exclusively bcrypt for password hashing, the abstraction layer provided by passlib is unnecessary and causes version conflicts.

docs.title: Positive
docs.text:
• No dependency conflicts between passlib and bcrypt
  • Fewer dependencies

docs.title: Negative
docs.text: • No passlib-compatible hash format abstraction (not needed)

docs.title: ADR-006: Embedding Model multilingual-e5-base and Hybrid Search for RAG
docs.text: Status: Accepted Date: 2026-03-30 Deciders: Kamerplanter Development Team

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
The Kamerplanter AI assistant (REQ-031) uses a RAG pipeline (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) to answer plant care questions from a curated knowledge base. The pipeline consists of:

  1. Embedding Service (ONNX Runtime) — generates vectors from text
  2. pgvector (PostgreSQL) — stores and searches vectors via cosine similarity
  3. LLM (Ollama/Claude) — generates answers from retrieved context chunks

docs.title: Problem: Retrieval quality was unusable
docs.text:
During RAG smoke testing with the previous embedding model paraphrase-multilingual-MiniLM-L12-v2 (384 dimensions), vector search for the question "My lower leaves are turning yellow, the upper ones are still green. What's missing?" returned:

Rank Chunk Score 1 Pre-harvest safety interval 0.7909 2 Topping and FIM — forcing branching 0.7867 3 Seed pre-treatment — stratification 0.7380 4 Flowering in vegetables and fruit 0.7346 5 Gray mold (Botrytis) detection 0.7345

The correct chunk "Nitrogen (N) Deficiency" — which exactly describes these symptoms — was completely absent from the top 5. All scores fell between 0.73 and 0.79 with virtually no differentiation. The LLM received irrelevant context and hallucinated a wrong diagnosis (potassium instead of nitrogen).

docs.title: Root cause
docs.text: paraphrase-multilingual-MiniLM-L12-v2 is a small model (118M parameters, 384 dimensions) optimized for general paraphrase detection. It encodes all German plant care texts into too narrow a region of the vector space — cosine similarity between completely different chunks ranges from 0.73 to 0.88. The vector space is too low-dimensional to capture domain-specific nuances.

docs.title: 1. Switch to multilingual-e5-base (768 dimensions)
docs.text:
We replace paraphrase-multilingual-MiniLM-L12-v2 with intfloat/multilingual-e5-base:

  • 278M parameters (vs. 118M) — double the model capacity
  • 768 dimensions (vs. 384) — double the vector resolution
  • E5 architecture requires prefixes: "query: " for search queries, "passage: " for documents. This asymmetric encoding significantly improves retrieval quality over symmetric models.
  • MTEB benchmark: E5-base substantially outperforms MiniLM-L12-v2 on multilingual retrieval tasks.

docs.title: Rejected alternative: multilingual-e5-large (1024 dimensions)
docs.text: Was implemented first but rejected because: - ONNX model is ~2.2 GB (split across model.onnx + model.onnx_data) - Docker build times exceeding 15 minutes for the download - Significantly higher RAM usage during inference (~2 GB vs. ~1 GB) - Quality gain from 768 to 1024 dimensions does not justify the cost for 241 chunks

docs.title: 2. Hybrid Search with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF)
docs.text:
Pure vector search is fragile when the embedding model does not encode domain terms well enough. We supplement the search with PostgreSQL full-text search (BM25) and fuse both rankings with RRF:

RRF_score = 0.5 / (60 + vector_rank) + 0.5 / (60 + text_rank)
  • Vector search: Semantic similarity — finds conceptually related content
  • Full-text search (tsvector with German stemmer): Keyword match — finds exact terms like "Stickstoff", "gelb", "untere Blaetter"
  • RRF fusion: Chunks ranking high in both systems are preferred

This requires: - New search_text tsvector column in ai_vector_chunks (GIN index) - Ingest pipeline populates search_text automatically from title || content

docs.title: Affected components
docs.text: Component Change docker/embedding-service/Dockerfile Model download intfloat/multilingual-e5-base docker/embedding-service/main.py prefix field in EmbedRequest, default model Migration 002 vector(384) to vector(768), IVFFlat to HNSW, search_text tsvector + GIN vector_chunk_repository.py New hybrid_search() method, search_text in upsert embedding_engine.py prefix parameter for query/passage distinction knowledge_ingestor.py prefix="passage: " when embedding documents knowledge_service.py prefix="query: " when embedding queries, hybrid_search tools/rag-eval/eval_rag.py Hybrid search + E5 prefix in standalone eval tool Helm values-dev EMBEDDING_MODEL environment variable Backend settings Default embedding_model

docs.title: Positive
docs.text:
• Significantly better retrieval quality through higher vector resolution and asymmetric query/passage encoding
  • Hybrid search as safety net: even when semantic search fails, keyword matches find the correct chunk
  • RRF is parameter-light and robust — no expensive tuning required
  • HNSW index scales better than IVFFlat at higher dimensions

docs.title: Negative
docs.text:
• Embedding service requires ~1 GB RAM instead of ~500 MB (acceptable for dev/prod)
  • Docker image ~1.5 GB instead of ~600 MB
  • All existing embeddings must be regenerated after migration 002 (reindex via Celery task)
  • E5 prefix convention must be consistently applied across all callers

docs.title: Neutral
docs.text:
• Chunk count (241) is small enough that the model switch has immediately measurable impact
  • PostgreSQL German stemmer handles domain terms like "Naehrstoffmangel" or "Ueberwaesserung" well

docs.title: Deployment Profiles
docs.text: Kamerplanter is modular by design. You decide which components to run — from a lean setup on a Raspberry Pi to a full multi-tenant deployment on Kubernetes. This page helps you find the right profile for your use case.

docs.title: Component Overview
docs.text: Every Kamerplanter installation consists of a core (always required) and optional components that you enable as needed.

docs.title: Core (always active)
docs.text: Component Purpose Backend (FastAPI) REST API, business logic, phase control, fertilization plans Frontend (React) Web interface ArangoDB Primary database (documents + graph queries) Valkey (Redis-compatible) Cache and Celery broker Celery Worker + Beat Background tasks (care reminders, data enrichment, AI tips)

docs.title: Optional Components
docs.text: Component Purpose Resource requirements Configuration Operating mode light = no login, single user; full = JWT auth, multi-tenant — KAMERPLANTER_MODE AI assistant Care tips, diagnostics, recommendations via language model 2--8 GB RAM (local) AI_DEFAULT_PROVIDER Ollama Local language model execution (no data leaves your network) 4--16 GB RAM, optional GPU Docker profile ollama Knowledge Service RAG pipeline: search knowledge base, enrich context 512 MB RAM Separate deployment VectorDB (pgvector) Vector store for RAG embeddings 256 MB RAM VECTORDB_ENABLED Embedding Service ONNX-based embedding computation (no PyTorch) 512 MB RAM Separate deployment Reranker Service Cross-encoder re-ranking for higher RAG precision (ADR-007) 1.5–4 GB RAM RERANKER_URL TimescaleDB Time-series sensor data, automatic downsampling 256--512 MB RAM TIMESCALEDB_ENABLED Home Assistant Sensor and actuator integration (temperature, humidity, lights) External HA_URL + HA_ACCESS_TOKEN External enrichment Auto-enrich plant data from GBIF and Perenual — PERENUAL_API_KEY

docs.title: Profiles at a Glance
docs.text:
The following matrix shows five predefined profiles. Each profile is a recommendation — you can always add or remove individual components.

Minimal Hobby Standard Professional SaaS Infrastructure Docker Compose Docker Compose Docker Compose / K8s Kubernetes Kubernetes Operating mode Light Light Full Full Full AI assistant — Ollama (local) Ollama (local) Ollama + cloud fallback Cloud (OpenAI / Anthropic) Knowledge Service + RAG — — Optional Yes Yes Reranker Service — — Optional Optional Yes TimescaleDB — — Optional Yes Yes Home Assistant — Optional Optional Yes Optional External enrichment — Optional Yes Yes Yes Celery Worker Yes Yes Yes Yes Yes Target audience Raspberry Pi, quick trial Hobby grower, home server Engaged hobbyists, small community gardens Indoor growing, large community gardens Managed hosting, multiple tenants Total RAM ~1 GB ~3 GB ~4 GB ~6 GB ~8 GB

docs.title: Target audience
docs.text: You want to try Kamerplanter quickly or only have a few houseplants. A Raspberry Pi ⅘ or an old laptop is sufficient. You need neither login nor AI.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Docker + Docker Compose
  • 1 GB free RAM, 2 GB disk space
  • Raspberry Pi 4 (2 GB), Raspberry Pi 5, NUC, laptop

docs.title: Active components
docs.text:
• Backend + Frontend
  • ArangoDB + Valkey
  • Celery Worker + Beat
  • AI assistant
  • TimescaleDB
  • Home Assistant
  • Knowledge Service / RAG

docs.title: Example configuration
docs.text:
docker-compose.yml (excerpt)
services:
  arangodb:
    image: arangodb:3.11
    # ...

  valkey:
    image: valkey/valkey:8-alpine
    # ...

  backend:
    build: ./src/backend
    environment:
      KAMERPLANTER_MODE: light
      AI_DEFAULT_PROVIDER: none
      TIMESCALEDB_ENABLED: "false"
      VECTORDB_ENABLED: "false"
    depends_on: [arangodb, valkey]

  celery-worker:
    build: ./src/backend
    command: celery -A app.tasks worker --loglevel=info
    depends_on: [arangodb, valkey]

  celery-beat:
    build: ./src/backend
    command: celery -A app.tasks beat --loglevel=info
    depends_on: [arangodb, valkey]

  frontend:
    build: ./src/frontend
    environment:
      KAMERPLANTER_MODE: light
    depends_on: [backend]

docs.title: What you gain by upgrading
docs.text: Without the AI assistant you get no automatic care tips or diagnostics. You can add Ollama at any time without losing data.

docs.title: Target audience
docs.text: You have 10--50 plants and a home server (NAS, old desktop, NUC). You want AI-powered care tips, but your data should not leave your network. You do not need login — you are the only user.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Docker + Docker Compose
  • 4 GB free RAM (8 GB with 7B model), optional GPU
  • Home server, NUC, desktop PC

docs.title: Active components
docs.text:
• Backend + Frontend
  • ArangoDB + Valkey
  • Celery Worker + Beat
  • Ollama (local language model)
  • Knowledge Service / RAG (optionally enabled)
  • TimescaleDB
  • Home Assistant (optional)
  • External enrichment (optional)

docs.title: Example configuration
docs.text:
docker-compose.yml (excerpt)
services:
  # ... core as in Minimal ...

  backend:
    build: ./src/backend
    environment:
      KAMERPLANTER_MODE: light
      AI_DEFAULT_PROVIDER: ollama
      AI_OLLAMA_URL: http://ollama:11434
      AI_OLLAMA_MODEL: gemma3:4b
      TIMESCALEDB_ENABLED: "false"
    depends_on: [arangodb, valkey]

  ollama:
    image: ollama/ollama:latest
    volumes:
      - ollama_models:/models
    # GPU passthrough (optional):
    # deploy:
    #   resources:
    #     reservations:
    #       devices:
    #         - capabilities: [gpu]

Model selection

Start with gemma3:4b — it runs on most machines from 2020 onwards without a GPU. For details on model selection, see AI Provider Setup.

docs.title: What you gain by upgrading
docs.text: Without full mode you cannot invite additional users. Without TimescaleDB, sensor data is not stored long-term. Both can be enabled later.

docs.title: Target audience
docs.text: You are an engaged hobbyist or run a small community garden. Multiple people should have their own accounts. You want AI tips and optionally store sensor data long-term.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Docker Compose or Kubernetes cluster
  • 4--6 GB free RAM
  • Server, NUC, or small K8s cluster

docs.title: Active components
docs.text:
• Backend + Frontend
  • ArangoDB + Valkey
  • Celery Worker + Beat
  • Ollama (local language model)
  • External enrichment (GBIF + Perenual)
  • Knowledge Service / RAG (optional)
  • TimescaleDB (optional)
  • Home Assistant (optional)

docs.title: Example configuration
docs.text:
Docker ComposeHelm Values docker-compose.yml (excerpt)
services:
  # ... core + Ollama ...

  backend:
    build: ./src/backend
    environment:
      KAMERPLANTER_MODE: full
      AI_DEFAULT_PROVIDER: ollama
      AI_OLLAMA_URL: http://ollama:11434
      AI_OLLAMA_MODEL: gemma3:4b
      JWT_SECRET_KEY: ${JWT_SECRET_KEY}  # openssl rand -hex 32
      PERENUAL_API_KEY: ${PERENUAL_API_KEY}
      TIMESCALEDB_ENABLED: ${TIMESCALEDB_ENABLED:-false}
    depends_on: [arangodb, valkey]
values.yaml (excerpt)
controllers:
  backend:
    containers:
      main:
        env:
          KAMERPLANTER_MODE: full
          AI_DEFAULT_PROVIDER: ollama
          AI_OLLAMA_URL: http://ollama:11434
          AI_OLLAMA_MODEL: gemma3:4b
          TIMESCALEDB_ENABLED: "false"

TimescaleDB only needed with sensors

If you do not plan to connect sensors or Home Assistant, you can skip TimescaleDB. Manual measurements (pH, EC) are stored in ArangoDB. TimescaleDB becomes useful only with automatic, high-frequency data collection.

docs.title: What you gain by upgrading
docs.text: Without TimescaleDB there is no automatic downsampling of sensor data. Without Home Assistant there is no automatic sensor collection or actuator control. Without the Knowledge Service / RAG there are no context-enriched AI responses from the knowledge base.

docs.title: Target audience
docs.text: You run professional indoor growing or a large community garden with role management. Sensors and actuators are connected via Home Assistant. You want seamless time-series storage, AI diagnostics with RAG context, and cloud fallback for the language model.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Kubernetes cluster (3+ nodes recommended)
  • 6--8 GB RAM for Kamerplanter pods
  • Home Assistant instance on the network
  • Optional: GPU node for faster AI inference

docs.title: Active components
docs.text:
• Backend + Frontend
  • ArangoDB + Valkey
  • Celery Worker + Beat
  • Ollama + cloud fallback (OpenAI or Anthropic)
  • Knowledge Service + VectorDB + Embedding Service
  • Reranker Service (cross-encoder re-ranking)
  • TimescaleDB
  • Home Assistant
  • External enrichment (GBIF + Perenual)

docs.title: Example configuration
docs.text:
values.yaml (excerpt)
controllers:
  backend:
    containers:
      main:
        env:
          KAMERPLANTER_MODE: full
          AI_DEFAULT_PROVIDER: ollama
          AI_OLLAMA_URL: http://ollama:11434
          AI_OLLAMA_MODEL: mistral:7b
          AI_FALLBACK_PROVIDER: openai
          AI_OPENAI_API_KEY:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: kamerplanter-secrets
              key: openai-api-key
          TIMESCALEDB_ENABLED: "true"
          TIMESCALEDB_HOST: timescaledb
          HA_URL: http://homeassistant.home:8123
          HA_ACCESS_TOKEN:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: kamerplanter-secrets
              key: ha-access-token
          PERENUAL_API_KEY:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: kamerplanter-secrets
              key: perenual-api-key

  timescaledb:
    enabled: true

  knowledge-service:
    enabled: true
    containers:
      main:
        env:
          RERANKER_URL: "http://kamerplanter-ki-reranker-service:8081"
          RERANKER_INITIAL_K: "20"
          RERANKER_TOP_K: "5"

  reranker-service:
    enabled: true
    containers:
      main:
        env:
          RERANKER_MODEL: "bge-reranker-v2-m3"

  embedding-service:
    enabled: true

  vectordb:
    enabled: true

Keep secrets out of values.yaml

API keys and tokens belong in Kubernetes Secrets or an external secret manager (e.g., Sealed Secrets, External Secrets Operator). Use secretKeyRef in the Helm values.

docs.title: What you gain by upgrading
docs.text: In the Professional profile you run a single instance for your organization. The SaaS profile adds multi-tenant isolation, horizontal scaling, and cloud AI as the primary provider.

docs.title: Target audience
docs.text: You operate Kamerplanter as a platform for multiple independent tenants (gardens, businesses, communities). Each tenant has its own data, roles, and settings. You need horizontal scaling and reliable cloud AI.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Kubernetes cluster with autoscaling
  • 8+ GB RAM for Kamerplanter pods
  • Managed database services recommended (ArangoDB Oasis, managed PostgreSQL)
  • Cloud AI provider account (OpenAI or Anthropic)

docs.title: Active components
docs.text:
• Backend + Frontend (multiple replicas)
  • ArangoDB + Valkey
  • Celery Worker (multiple replicas) + Beat
  • Cloud AI (OpenAI / Anthropic)
  • Knowledge Service + VectorDB + Embedding Service
  • TimescaleDB
  • External enrichment (GBIF + Perenual)
  • Home Assistant (optional, tenant-specific)

docs.title: Example configuration
docs.text:
values.yaml (excerpt)
controllers:
  backend:
    replicas: 3
    containers:
      main:
        env:
          KAMERPLANTER_MODE: full
          AI_DEFAULT_PROVIDER: openai
          AI_OPENAI_MODEL: gpt-4o-mini
          TIMESCALEDB_ENABLED: "true"

  celery-worker:
    replicas: 2

  frontend:
    replicas: 2

Managed databases

In SaaS operation, consider using managed database services instead of self-hosted containers. This significantly reduces operational overhead for backups, updates, and high availability.

docs.title: Build Your Own Profile
docs.text:
The profiles above are recommendations. You can enable or disable any component individually by setting the corresponding environment variables:

Decision Variable Values Login and multi-tenant? KAMERPLANTER_MODE light / full AI care tips? AI_DEFAULT_PROVIDER ollama, llamacpp, openai, anthropic, openai-compatible, none Sensor time-series? TIMESCALEDB_ENABLED true / false RAG knowledge base? VECTORDB_ENABLED true / false Re-ranking (higher precision)? RERANKER_URL HTTP URL of the reranker service (empty = disabled) Home Assistant? HA_URL + HA_ACCESS_TOKEN URL + token (empty = disabled) Plant data enrichment? PERENUAL_API_KEY API key (empty = GBIF only)

In Docker Compose, enable optional services via profiles:

# Core only:
docker compose up -d

# With Ollama and TimescaleDB:
docker compose --profile ollama --profile timescaledb up -d

# With RAG (Knowledge Service + VectorDB + Reranker):
docker compose --profile ollama --profile timescaledb --profile vectordb up -d

Reranker in the Docker Compose vectordb profile

The reranker-service is assigned to the vectordb Docker Compose profile and starts together with the Knowledge Service and VectorDB. The reranker is more resource-intensive than the embedding service and VectorDB — on low-powered hardware you can leave RERANKER_URL empty to use hybrid search without re-ranking.

For a complete list of all environment variables, see Environment Variables.

docs.title: Decision Guide
docs.text:
The following flowchart helps you find a suitable profile:

flowchart TD
    A[How many users?] -->|Just me| B{Do you need AI tips?}
    A -->|2-10 people| D{Kubernetes available?}
    A -->|10+ / tenants| G[SaaS / Multi-Tenant]

    B -->|No| C[Minimal]
    B -->|Yes| E[Hobby]

    D -->|No| F[Standard\nDocker Compose]
    D -->|Yes| H{Sensors / HA?}

    H -->|No| F2[Standard\nKubernetes]
    H -->|Yes| I[Professional]

docs.title: Can I upgrade to a larger profile later?
docs.text: Yes. All profiles use the same database. You can add components at any time (e.g., enable Ollama, start TimescaleDB, switch from light to full mode) without losing data. When switching from light to full mode, you need to set a password for the existing system user once.

docs.title: Can I run Ollama on a Raspberry Pi?
docs.text: Yes, starting with the Raspberry Pi 5 with 8 GB RAM. Use a small model like llama3.2:3b. Response times are 15--30 seconds per tip — acceptable but not fast. The Raspberry Pi 4 does not have sufficient performance for larger models.

docs.title: Do I need TimescaleDB without sensors?
docs.text: No. Without automatic sensor data collection (IoT/MQTT or Home Assistant), TimescaleDB provides no benefit. Manual measurements (pH, EC) are stored in ArangoDB. You can enable TimescaleDB later when you connect sensors.

docs.title: What happens if I do not configure an AI provider?
docs.text: Kamerplanter works fully without AI. When AI_DEFAULT_PROVIDER=none is set (or no provider is configured), the AI tip cards are not shown in the interface. All rule-based features (phase control, fertilization plans, care reminders) operate independently of the AI provider.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Light Mode — Details on running without authentication
  • AI Provider Setup — Configure Ollama, OpenAI, Anthropic, and other providers
  • Home Assistant Integration — Sensor and actuator integration
  • Environment Variables — Complete variable reference
  • Kubernetes — Cluster setup and deployment
  • Infrastructure — Skaffold Profiles — Skaffold modules (-m ki) for the AI stack

docs.title: Discover Features
docs.text: Kamerplanter has many features -- but you don't need to learn them all at once. Depending on your experience level, Kamerplanter shows or hides features so you don't feel overwhelmed.

docs.title: For Beginners
docs.text: These features are visible right away and are more than enough to get started:

docs.title: Manage Plants
docs.text:
Create new plants, edit them, or remove them. Each plant has a species (e.g. "Basil") and is assigned to a location (e.g. "Kitchen window").

Learn more: Plant Master Data

docs.title: Set Up Locations
docs.text:
Describe where your plants are. Locations can be nested -- e.g. "Apartment > Living Room > South Windowsill".

Learn more: Locations & Substrates

docs.title: Complete Tasks
docs.text:
Kamerplanter automatically creates tasks like "Water", "Repot", or "Fertilize". You see what's due today and check off completed tasks.

Learn more: Tasks

docs.title: Care Reminders
docs.text:
Automatic reminders for watering, fertilizing, and other care steps. Kamerplanter adjusts intervals to the season and your plant's needs.

Learn more: Care Reminders

docs.title: Dashboard
docs.text:
Your personal cockpit: open tasks, plant status, and care reminders at a glance.

Learn more: Dashboard

docs.title: For Intermediate Users
docs.text: When you're ready, switch your experience level to Intermediate. These features become available:

docs.title: Fertilization & Nutrients
docs.text:
Create nutrient plans, calculate nutrient solutions, and track EC values. Kamerplanter knows common fertilizer products and calculates mixing ratios.

Learn more: Fertilization Logic

docs.title: Watering Log
docs.text:
Document each watering event with volume, EC, and pH. Kamerplanter shows you trends and warns about deviations.

Learn more: Watering Log

docs.title: Tank Management
docs.text:
Manage nutrient solution tanks with fill level, EC, and pH. Ideal when you're feeding multiple plants from the same solution.

Learn more: Tank Management

docs.title: Calendar
docs.text:
All dates and tasks in a calendar view. Exportable as an iCal feed for your calendar app.

Learn more: Calendar

docs.title: Growth Phases
docs.text:
Track which phase your plants are in (germination, vegetative, flowering, harvest) and change phases manually or automatically.

Learn more: Growth Phases

docs.title: For Experts
docs.text: At the Expert level, all features are available:

docs.title: Pest Management (IPM)
docs.text:
Integrated pest management with pest/disease tracking, treatment logs, waiting periods, and resistance management.

Learn more: Pest Management (IPM)

docs.title: Planting Runs
docs.text:
Group plants into runs -- ideal for seasonal growing cycles or multiple tents.

Learn more: Planting Runs

docs.title: Harvest
docs.text:
Record harvest indicators, document yields, and assess quality. With automatic waiting period checks.

Learn more: Harvest

docs.title: Sensors
docs.text:
Connect sensors -- manually, via Home Assistant, or via MQTT. Kamerplanter displays VPD, temperature, humidity, and more.

Learn more: Sensors

docs.title: Actuator Control
docs.text:
Control fans, lights, and pumps via Home Assistant or MQTT. Rule-based control with hysteresis and safety limits.

Learn more: Actuator Control

docs.title: Propagation
docs.text:
Track cuttings, sowings, and crosses. Kamerplanter maintains a genetic family tree across generations.

Learn more: Propagation

docs.title: AI Assistant
docs.text:
Ask questions about plant care and get answers based on the integrated knowledge base.

Learn more: AI Assistant

docs.title: Change your experience level
docs.text:
You can switch levels at any time:

  1. Click your profile picture (top right)
  2. Select Account Settings
  3. Go to the Experience Level tab
  4. Choose your desired level

Don't worry: switching levels never deletes data. Kamerplanter only shows or hides UI elements.

docs.title: More features
docs.text: Feature Description More Tenants & Gardens Manage multiple gardens, invite members Tenants Privacy (GDPR) View, export, or delete your data Privacy Print & Export Print plant cards and lists Print & Export Light Mode Use Kamerplanter without login (single user) Light Mode Repeat Onboarding Restart the wizard, add another starter kit Onboarding

docs.title: Your First Plant
docs.text: This guide walks you step by step from opening Kamerplanter for the first time to having your first plant -- complete with a watering schedule and tasks.

docs.title: 1. Start the Onboarding Wizard
docs.text:
When you open Kamerplanter for the first time, the Onboarding Wizard greets you. It guides you through setup in five short steps.

Already completed the wizard?

You can restart the wizard at any time: Go to Account Settings and click Restart Onboarding Wizard.

docs.title: 2. Choose your experience level
docs.text:
In the first step, choose how much experience you have with plant care:

Level What you see Beginner Only the core features: plants, locations, tasks, watering Intermediate Plus fertilization, tanks, sensors, and calendar Expert All features -- including IPM, VPD, EC tracking, and propagation

You can change the level at any time without losing data. Kamerplanter only hides UI elements -- your data remains fully intact.

docs.title: 3. Describe your environment
docs.text:
Briefly describe where your plants are. For example:

  • "Kitchen window, south-facing"
  • "Balcony, partial shade"
  • "Grow tent 120x120"

Kamerplanter uses this info to suggest suitable starter kits.

docs.title: 4. Choose a Starter Kit
docs.text:
A starter kit is a pre-configured package with plant species, locations, and matching settings. Just pick the one that fits you best:

Starter Kit What you get Windowsill Herbs Basil, parsley, chives -- perfect for getting started Houseplant Starter Monstera, pothos, peace lily -- the most popular houseplants Pet-Friendly Houseplants Only non-toxic plants for homes with pets Balcony Tomatoes Tomato, pepper -- balcony vegetables for beginners Succulents & Cacti Low-maintenance succulents Mediterranean Herbs Rosemary, thyme, oregano Balcony Chillies Various chilli varieties for the balcony Vegetable Bed Classic vegetable bed with companion planting Indoor Grow Tent Controlled indoor growing with phase management

No matching kit?

No problem -- you can always add your own plants after the wizard. The kits are just a quick start.

docs.title: 5. Set plant counts & favorites
docs.text:
In the next step, you see the plants from your chosen kit. Here you can:

  • Adjust the count per plant type (e.g. 3 basil instead of 1)
  • Mark plants as favorites -- these appear at the top of your dashboard

docs.title: 6. Review and finish
docs.text:
Review the summary and click Finish. Kamerplanter now automatically creates:

  • Your plants with matching growth phases
  • A location based on your environment description
  • Initial tasks (e.g. "Water basil", "Check location")

docs.title: 7. Your Dashboard
docs.text:
After the wizard, you land on the Dashboard. Here you see at a glance:

  • Upcoming tasks -- what needs to be done today
  • Your plants -- with current status and phase
  • Care reminders -- when the next watering round is due

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
You have your first plant -- now what?

What you want to do Where to find it Water a plant and log it Watering Log Add more plants Plant Master Data Rename or add locations Locations & Substrates View and complete tasks Tasks See all features at a glance Discover Features
